results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born May 31, 1931, in Budapest, Hungary) is a pioneering Hungarian-born American conceptual, ecological, and land artist based in New York City. She rose to international prominence in the late 1960s and 1970s for her multi-disciplinary approach, which integrates science, philosophy, mathematics, linguistics, and ecology. Denes is widely regarded as a founding figure of the environmental art movement, notably for her work 'Rice/Tree/Burial' (1968), recognized as one of the first site-specific ecological performance pieces. During the early 1970s, she was a founding member of the A.I.R. Gallery, the first artist-run cooperative gallery for women in the United States. Her work from this period includes the 'Philosophical Drawings' (1969–80), the 'Pyramid Series' (1970–), and 'Map Projections' (1973–79). Denes has a history of collaborating with technological and scientific institutions; she participated in the landmark 'Software' exhibition at the Jewish Museum in 1970, where she worked with the computer scientist group R.E.S.I.S.T.O.R.S. to produce computational experiments. Her association with technology-driven art is further evidenced by her attendance at AT&T Bell Laboratories—the institution where the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization was rooted—where she experimented with X-ray technology, computer animation, and early digital matrices. She is perhaps best known for 'Wheatfield — A Confrontation' (1982), a monumental two-acre field of wheat she planted on a landfill in lower Manhattan near Wall Street and the World Trade Center.
